sections.label: Section 217
sections.original: ὥστ ’ ἐκπιεῖν γέ σ ’, ἢν θέλῃς , ὅλον πίθον .
sections.translation: Aye , so that thou canst swill off a whole hogshead , so it please thee .

sections.label: Section 218
sections.original: μήλειον ἢ βόειον ἢ μεμιγμένον ;
sections.translation: Sheeps ’ milk or cows ’ milk or a mixture of both ?

sections.label: Section 219
sections.original: ὧν ἂν θέλῃς σύ , μὴ ’ μὲ καταπίῃς μόνον .
sections.translation: Whichever thou wilt ; don ’ t swallow me , that ’ s all .

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: ἥκιστ ’· ἐπεί μ ’ ἂν ἐν μέσῃ τῇ γαστέρι
sections.translation: Not I ; for you would start kicking in the pit of my stomach and kill me by your antics . ( Catching sight of Odysseus and his followers .) Ha ! what is this crowd I see near the folds ? Some pirates or robbers have put in here . Yes , I really see the lambs from my caves

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: στρεπταῖς λύγοισι σῶμα συμπεπλεγμένους ,
sections.translation: tied up there with twisted osiers , cheese-presses scattered about , and old Silenus with his bald pate all swollen with blows .

sections.label: Section 228
sections.original: ὤμοι , πυρέσσω συγκεκομμένος τάλας .
sections.translation: Oh ! oh ! poor wretch that I am , pounded to a fever .

sections.label: Section 229
sections.original: ὑπὸ τοῦ ; τίς ἐς σὸν κρᾶτ ’ ἐπύκτευσεν , γέρον ;
sections.translation: By whom ? who has been pounding thy head , old sirrah ?

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: ὑπὸ τῶνδε , Κύκλωψ , ὅτι τὰ σ ’ οὐκ εἴων φέρειν .
sections.translation: These are the culprits , Cyclops , all because I refused to let them plunder thee .

sections.label: Section 231
sections.original: οὐκ ᾖσαν ὄντα θεόν με καὶ θεῶν ἄπο ;
sections.translation: Did they not know I was a god and sprung from gods ?

sections.label: Section 232
sections.original: ἔλεγον ἐγὼ τάδ ’· οἳ δ ’ ἐφόρουν τὰ χρήματα ·
sections.translation: That was what I told them , but they persisted in plundering thy goods , and , in spite of my efforts , they actually began to eat the cheese and carry off the lambs ; and they said they would

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: κλῳῷ τριπήχει , κᾆτα τὸν ὀφθαλμὸν μέσον
sections.translation: tie thee in a three-cubit pillory and tear out thy bowels by force at thy navel , and flay thy back thoroughly with the scourge ; and then , after binding thee , fling thy carcase down among the benches of their ship to sell to some one

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: πέτρους μοχλεύειν , ἢ ’ ς μυλῶνα καταβαλεῖν .
sections.translation: for heaving up stones , or else throw thee into a mill .

sections.label: Section 241
sections.original: ἄληθες ; οὔκουν κοπίδας ὡς τάχιστ ’ ἰὼν
sections.translation: Oh , indeed ! Be off then and sharpen my cleavers at once ; heap high the faggots and light them ; for they shall be slain forthwith and fill this maw of mine ,

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: θερμὴν ἔδοντος δαῖτα τῷ κρεανόμῳ ,
sections.translation: what time I pick my feast hot from the coals , waiting not for carvers , and fish up the rest from the cauldron boiled and sodden ; for I have had my fill of mountain-fare and sated myself with banquets of lions and stags , but ’ tis long I have been without human flesh .

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: τὰ καινά γ ’ ἐκ τῶν ἠθάδων , ὦ δέσποτα ,
sections.translation: Truly , master , a change like this is all the sweeter after everyday fare ; for just of late there have been no fresh arrivals of strangers at these caves .

sections.label: Section 253
sections.original: Κύκλωψ , ἄκουσον ἐν μέρει καὶ τῶν ξένων .
sections.translation: Hear the strangers too in turn , Cyclops . We had come near the cave from our ship ,

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: σῶν ἆσσον ἄντρων ἤλθομεν νεὼς ἄπο .
sections.translation: wishing to procure provisions by purchase , when this fellow sold us the lambs and handed them over for a stoup of wine to drink himself ,— a voluntary act on both sides ,— there was no violence employed at all . No , there is not a particle of truth in the story he tells ,

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: ἐπεὶ κατελήφθη σοῦ λάθρᾳ πωλῶν τὰ σά .
sections.translation: now that he has been caught selling thy property behind thy back .

sections.label: Section 261
sections.original: ἐγώ ; κακῶς γὰρ ἐξόλοι ’.
sections.translation: I ? Perdition catch thee !

sections.label: Section 261b
sections.original: εἰ ψεύδομαι .
sections.translation: If I am lying , yes .

sections.label: Section 262
sections.original: μὰ τὸν Ποσειδῶ τὸν τεκόντα σ ’, ὦ Κύκλωψ ,
sections.translation: O Cyclops , by thy sire Poseidon , by mighty Triton and Nereus , by Calypso and the daughters of Nereus ,

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: τά θ ’ ἱερὰ κύματ ’ ἰχθύων τε πᾶν γένος ,
sections.translation: by the sacred billows and all the race of fishes ! I swear to thee , most noble sir , dear little Cyclops , master mine , it is not I who sell thy goods to strangers , else may these children , dearly as I love them , come to an evil end .

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: αὐτὸς ἔχ ’. ἔγωγε τοῖς ξένοις τὰ χρήματα
sections.translation: Keep that for thyself ; with my own eyes I saw thee sell the goods to the strangers ; and if I lie , perdition catch my sire ! but injure not the strangers .

sections.label: Section 273
sections.original: ψεύδεσθ ’· ἔγωγε τῷδε τοῦ Ῥαδαμάνθυος
sections.translation: Ye lie ; for my part I put more faith in him than Rhadamanthus , declaring him more just .

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: θέλω δ ’ ἐρέσθαι · πόθεν ἐπλεύσατ ’, ὦ ξένοι ;
sections.translation: But I have some questions to ask . Whence sailed ye , strangers ? of what country are you ? what city was it nursed your childhood ?

sections.label: Section 277
sections.original: Ἰθακήσιοι μὲν τὸ γένος , Ἰλίου δ ’ ἄπο ,
sections.translation: We are Ithacans by birth , and have been driven from our course by the winds of the sea on our way from Ilium , after sacking its citadel .

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: ἦ τῆς κακίστης οἳ μετήλθεθ ’ ἁρπαγὰς
sections.translation: Are ye the men who visited on Ilium , that bordereth on Scamander ’ s wave , the rape of Helen , worst of women ?

sections.label: Section 282
sections.original: οὗτοι , πόνον τὸν δεινὸν ἐξηντληκότες .
sections.translation: We are ; that was the fearful labour we endured .

sections.label: Section 283
sections.original: αἰσχρὸν στράτευμά γ ’, οἵτινες μιᾶς χάριν
sections.translation: A sorry expedition yours , to have sailed to the land of Phrygia for the sake of one woman !

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: θεοῦ τὸ πρᾶγμα · μηδέν ’ αἰτιῶ βροτῶν .
sections.translation: It was a god ’ s doing ; blame not any son of man . But thee do we implore , most noble son of Ocean ’ s god , speaking as free-born men ; be not so cruel as to slay thy friends on their coming to thy cave , nor regard us as food for thy jaws , an impious meal ;

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: οἳ τὸν σόν , ὦναξ , πατέρ ’ ἔχειν νεῶν ἕδρας
sections.translation: for we preserved thy sire , O king , in possession of his temple-seats deep in the nooks of Hellas ; and the sacred port of Taenarus and Malea ’ s furthest coves remain unharmed ; and Sunium ’ s rock , the silver-veined , sacred to Zeus-born Athena , still is safe ,

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: Γεραίστιοί τε καταφυγαί · τὰ θ ’ Ἑλλάδος
sections.translation: and Geraestus , the harbour of refuge ; and we did not permit Phrygians to put such an intolerable reproach on Hellas . [*] Now in these things thou too hast a share , for thou dwellest in a corner of the land of Hellas beneath Aetna ’ s fire-streaming rock ; and although thou turn from arguments ,

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: ἱκέτας δέχεσθαι ποντίους ἐφθαρμένους
sections.translation: still it is a custom amongst mortal men to receive shipwrecked sailors as their suppliants and show them hospitality and help them with raiment ; not that these should fill thy jaws and belly , their limbs transfixed with spits for piercing ox-flesh . The land of Priam hath emptied Hellas quite enough ,

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: πολλῶν νεκρῶν πιοῦσα δοριπετῆ φόνον ,
sections.translation: drinking the blood of many whom the spear laid low , with the ruin it has brought on widowed wives , on aged childless dames , and hoary-headed sires ; and if thou roast and consume the remnant ,— a meal thou wilt rue ,— why , where shall one turn ? Nay , be persuaded by me , Cyclops ;

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: πάρες τὸ μάργον σῆς γνάθου , τὸ δ ’ εὐσεβὲς
sections.translation: forego thy ravenous greed and choose piety rather than wickedness ; for on many a man ere now unrighteous gains have brought down retribution .

sections.label: Section 313
sections.original: παραινέσαι σοι βούλομαι · τῶν γὰρ κρεῶν
sections.translation: . . . . . I will give thee a word of advice ! as for his flesh , leave not a morsel of it , and if thou eat his tongue ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: κομψὸς γενήσῃ καὶ λαλίστατος , Κύκλωψ .
sections.translation: Cyclops , thou wilt become a monstrous clever talker .

sections.label: Section 316
sections.original: ὁ πλοῦτος , ἀνθρωπίσκε , τοῖς σοφοῖς θεός ,
sections.translation: Wealth , manikin , is the god for the wise ; all else is mere vaunting and fine words . Plague take the headlands by the sea , on which my father seats himself ! Why hast thou put forward these arguments ?

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: Ζηνὸς δ ’ ἐγὼ κεραυνὸν οὐ φρίσσω , ξένε ,
sections.translation: I shudder not at Zeus ’ s thunder , nor know I wherein Zeus is a mightier god than I , sir stranger ; what is more , I reck not of him ; my reasons hear . When he pours down the rain from above , here in this rock in quarters snug ,

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ἢ μόσχον ὀπτὸν ἤ τι θήρειον δάκος
sections.translation: feasting on roast calf ’ s flesh or some wild game and moistening well my upturned paunch with deep draughts from a tub of milk , I rival the thunderclaps of Zeus with my artillery ; and when the north-wind blows from Thrace and sheddeth snow ,

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: δοραῖσι θηρῶν σῶμα περιβαλὼν ἐμὸν
sections.translation: I wrap my carcase in the hides of beasts and light a fire , and what care I for snow ? The earth perforce , whether she like it or not , produces grass and fattens my flocks , which I sacrifice to no one save myself

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: καὶ τῇ μεγίστῃ , γαστρὶ τῇδε , δαιμόνων .
sections.translation: and this belly , the greatest of deities ; but to the gods , not I ! For surely to eat and drink one ’ s fill from day to day and give oneself no grief at all , this is the king of gods for your wise man , but lawgivers go hang , chequering , as they do , the life of man !

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: κλαίειν ἄνωγα · τὴν δ ’ ἐμὴν ψυχὴν ἐγὼ
sections.translation: And so I will not cease from indulging myself by devouring thee ; and thou shalt receive this stranger ’ s gift , that I may be free of blame ,— fire and my father ’ s element yonder , and a cauldron to hold thy flesh and boil it nicely in collops .

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἕρπετ ’ εἴσω , τῷ κατ ’ αὔλιον θεῷ
sections.translation: So in with you , that ye may feast me well , standing round the altar to honour the cavern ’ s god . [ Enters his cave .

sections.label: Section 347
sections.original: αἰαῖ , πόνους μὲν Τρωικοὺς ὑπεξέδυν
sections.translation: Alas ! escaped from the troubles of Troy and the sea , my barque now strands upon the whim and forbidding heart of this savage .

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: ὦ Παλλάς , ὦ δέσποινα Διογενὲς θεά ,
sections.translation: O Pallas , mistress mine , goddess-daughter of Zeus , help me , help me now ; for I am come to toils and depths of peril worse than all at Ilium ; and thou , O Zeus , the stranger ’ s god , who hast thy dwelling ’ mid the radiant stars , behold these things ;

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: ἄλλως νομίζῃ Ζεὺς τὸ μηδὲν ὢν θεός .
sections.translation: for , if thou regard them not , in vain art thou esteemed the great god Zeus , though but a thing of naught . [ Follows the Cyclops reluctantly .

sections.label: Section 356
sections.original: Εὐρείας φάρυγγος , ὦ Κύκλωψ ,
sections.translation: Ope wide the portal of thy gaping throat , Cyclops ; for strangers ’ limbs , both boiled and grilled , are ready from off the coals for thee to gnaw and tear and mince up small ,

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: δασυμάλλῳ ἐν αἰγίδι κλινομένῳ .
sections.translation: reclining in thy shaggy goatskin coat .

sections.label: Section 361
sections.original: Μή μοι μὴ προσδίδου ·
sections.translation: Relinquish not thy meal for me ; keep that boat for thyself alone . [*] Avaunt this cave ! avaunt the burnt-offerings ,

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: ἀποβώμιος ἃν ἔχει θυσίαν
sections.translation: which the godless Cyclops offers on Aetna ’ s altars , exulting in meals on strangers ’ flesh !

sections.label: Section 369
sections.original: Νηλής , ὦ τλᾶμον , ὅστις δωμάτων
sections.translation: Oh ! the ruthless monster ! to sacrifice his guests at his own hearth , the suppliants of his halls , cleaving and tearing and serving up to his loathsome teeth a feast of human flesh , hot from the coals .

sections.label: Section 374b
sections.original: Μή μοι μὴ προσδίδου ·

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ , τί λέξω , δείν ’ ἰδὼν ἄντρων ἔσω
sections.translation: ( reappearing with a look of horror .) O Zeus ! what can I say after the hideous sights I have seen inside the cave , things past belief , resembling more the tales men tell than aught they do ?

sections.label: Section 377
sections.original: τί δ ’ ἔστ ’, Ὀδυσσεῦ ; μῶν τεθοίναται σέθεν
sections.translation: What news , Odysseus ? has the Cyclops , most godless monster , been feasting on thy dear comrades ?

sections.label: Section 379
sections.original: δισσούς γ ’ ἀθρήσας κἀπιβαστάσας χεροῖν ,
sections.translation: Aye , he singled out a pair , on whom the flesh was

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: οἳ σαρκὸς εἶχον εὐτραφέστατον πάχος .
sections.translation: fattest and in best condition , and took them up in his hand to weigh .

sections.label: Section 381
sections.original: πῶς , ὦ ταλαίπωρ ’, ἦτε πάσχοντες τάδε ;
sections.translation: How went it with you then , poor wretch ?

sections.label: Section 382
sections.original: ἐπεὶ πετραίαν τήνδ ’ ἐσήλθομεν χθόνα ,
sections.translation: When we had entered yonder rocky abode , he lighted first a fire , throwing logs of towering oak upon his spacious hearth ,

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: τρισσῶν ἁμαξῶν ὡς ἀγώγιμον βάρος .
sections.translation: enough for three waggons to carry as their load ; next , close by the blazing flame , he placed his couch of pine-boughs laid upon the floor , and filled a bowl of some ten firkins , pouring white milk thereinto , after he had milked his kine ;
